metadata.seed: That I'm not the one in the wrong. You know, I. I'm extremely good at my job. I mean, I am the best employee that they have ever had. Not just currently, just. I mean, just ever. I make no errors. So how can I be wrong when it comes to these circumstances with my coworkers?

patient.query: That I'm not the one in the wrong. You know, I. I'm extremely good at my job. I mean, I am the best employee that they have ever had. Not just currently, just. I mean, just ever. I make no errors. So how can I be wrong when it comes to these circumstances with my coworkers?

automatic_thoughts: I'm not the one in the wrong
I'm extremely good at my job
I am the best employee
I make no errors
How can I be wrong?

llm_response.response: Your confidence in your work is evident. Can you describe a particular moment with a coworker that made you question that certainty? What evidence do you have that supports or challenges the idea that you might be wrong in that situation?

patient.query: Um, there was a meeting last month where I presented my ideas, and I thought I really had it all figured out. But then a coworker sharply criticized my suggestions, and it just felt like the ground was pulled from under me. I remember feeling embarrassed and doubting myself, like maybe I was out of my depth after all. It’s strange how one comment can stick with you, right? I guess I just keep replaying it in my head, wondering if I really know what I

automatic_thoughts: I thought I really had it all figured out
maybe I was out of my depth
one comment can stick with you
wondering if I really know what I

llm_response.response: It sounds like that criticism really shook your confidence, leaving you feeling embarrassed and questioning whether you’re truly capable. You’re replaying the moment in your mind, which can make the doubt feel even stronger. It’s understandable that one sharp comment can stick with you so vividly.

patient.query: Yeah, it’s like I can’t shake that moment. I keep thinking maybe I should have defended my ideas better or even just done them differently. I mean, there are times when I feel great about what I can contribute, but then something like this happens, and I just feel so small. It’s exhausting.

automatic_thoughts: I should have defended my ideas better
I should have done them differently

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of “what if” thoughts after that moment. What evidence do you have that you actually didn’t defend your ideas well, or that you could have done things differently? And when you think about those moments, what other ways might you interpret what happened that could feel less exhausting?

patient.query: I guess it’s just, when he criticized me, it felt so definitive, like I was being told I was wrong, and that really stung. It’s hard to see it in any other way, like I just keep thinking maybe I didn’t prepare enough or maybe my ideas really didn’t make sense. I do have moments where I think maybe it was just his opinion and not everyone feels that way, but that thought quickly fades when I replay how I felt. It just feels like I

automatic_thoughts: I was wrong
maybe I didn’t prepare enough
maybe my ideas really didn’t make sense
maybe it was just his opinion

llm_response.response: It sounds like the criticism really struck a nerve for you. What evidence do you have that the comment was definitive or that you were wrong? Could there be other ways to interpret what happened that might feel less painful?

patient.query: It’s just, when he criticized me, it felt so definitive, like I was being told I was wrong, and that really stung. I keep thinking maybe I didn’t prepare enough or my ideas just didn’t make sense. I have moments where I remind myself it was just his opinion, but that thought fades fast when I replay how I felt. It really hits me hard.

automatic_thoughts: I didn’t prepare enough
my ideas didn’t make sense
it was just his opinion

llm_response.response: It sounds like the criticism struck you deeply, leaving you questioning whether you were truly unprepared. What evidence supports the idea that you didn’t prepare enough, and what evidence might suggest otherwise? How might you view the situation if you considered that the critique could have been about the idea rather than your preparation?

patient.query: I mean, I thought I had done enough research and put in the effort. But then I start second-guessing everything, like maybe I missed something obvious or just didn’t think it through fully. It’s tough to separate my worth from the criticism, you know? I know it should be about the idea and not me, but in the moment, it felt really personal. Maybe if I looked at it differently, it would hurt less, but it’s hard to do that when I’m

automatic_thoughts: I missed something obvious
I didn’t think it through fully
It felt really personal

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling a lot of anxiety and hurt when you think the criticism is personal. What evidence do you have that the feedback is about you as a person, and what evidence might suggest it’s really about the idea itself?

patient.query: I thought I had done enough research and put in the effort. But then I start second-guessing everything, like maybe I missed something obvious or just didn’t think it through fully. It’s tough to separate my worth from the criticism, you know? I know it should be about the idea and not me, but in the moment, it felt so personal. It’s hard to see it any other way when I’m feeling this way.

automatic_thoughts: I missed something obvious
I didn’t think it through fully
It’s personal

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of pressure and doubt after all the effort you’ve put in. Feeling criticism as personal when you’re invested in the outcome is a common experience, and it’s understandable that it can feel overwhelming. You’re not alone in this; many people find themselves second‑guessing and questioning their worth in similar moments.

patient.query: Yeah, I guess it does feel like a lot sometimes. It’s just frustrating when I put myself out there and it feels like that one moment overshadows everything else. I mean, I know I’ve had good ideas before, but then I think, what if this is just how it’s going to be now? It’s like I can’t help but wonder if I’ll always feel this way after sharing my thoughts. It’s such a heavy weight.

automatic_thoughts: that one moment overshadows everything else
what if this is just how it’s going to be now?
I’ll always feel this way after sharing my thoughts

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re worried that every time you share, it will feel heavy. What evidence do you have that this always happens? Are there moments when sharing felt lighter or even helpful?

patient.query: I guess there have been times when I've shared and it went really well, like when a few colleagues actually built on my ideas and we created something great together. Those moments do feel lighter, and I can see the value in collaboration. But then I think, what if I only remember the bad experiences? It’s like those negative moments overshadow the good ones. It’s hard to shake off that weight, you know?

automatic_thoughts: what if I only remember the bad experiences?
negative moments overshadow the good ones

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re noticing a pattern where the negative memories feel more prominent. What evidence do you have that the bad experiences truly outweigh the good ones? How might you describe the balance between those positive collaborations and the challenges you’ve faced?

patient.query: Yeah, it’s like the bad stuff just clings to me more. I can think of those times when my ideas came together and it felt great, but then it’s almost like they fade into the background when I compare them to the criticism. I guess it’s easier to remember the moments when I felt small or doubt myself. It’s frustrating because I want to appreciate the good times, but the challenges just seem to echo louder. It’s hard to find that balance in my mind.

automatic_thoughts: bad stuff clings to me
good times fade into the background
I feel small
I doubt myself

llm_response.response: I hear how frustrating it feels when the negative moments seem to stay in the foreground while the good ones slip away. It’s common for criticism and self‑doubt to feel louder than the successes we’ve had. What’s one small thing you could do to bring a positive moment back into focus when you notice the negative thoughts rising?
